title: Isaiah 37
subtitle: The Angel of the LORD Strikes 185,000

sections.header: Verses 1–20 — “Hezekiah Spreads the Letter Before the LORD”

heb.paragraph: "Hezekiah received the letter and read it. Then he went up to the temple and spread it out (pāras) before the LORD" (v.14). Hezekiah tears his clothes, puts on sackcloth, goes to the temple, and sends for Isaiah (vv.1–2). Isaiah’s response: "Do not be afraid. This is what the LORD says: I will put a spirit in him so that he will hear a report and return to his own country" (vv.6–7). Sennacherib sends a taunting letter (vv.10–13). Hezekiah’s response is the model prayer of crisis: he physically spreads the letter before God’s face (v.14). His prayer: "You alone are God over all kingdoms. Give ear and hear; open your eyes and see" (vv.16, 17). "Deliver us so that all kingdoms may know that you alone, LORD, are God" (v.20).

notes.note: "Hezekiah tore his clothes and put on sackcloth and went into the temple." The right response to crisis: mourning, humility, and the house of God. Not the war room but the temple.
"Hezekiah spread it out before the LORD. And Hezekiah prayed." The physical act: unrolling the threatening letter in God’s presence. The most vivid prayer-posture in the OT.
"Deliver us so that all kingdoms may know that you alone are God." The prayer’s climax: not "save us for our sake" but "save us so the world knows YOU."

refs.note: The parallel account preserves Hezekiah's prayer nearly verbatim, highlighting its importance in both prophetic and historical traditions.
The communal lament asks God to remember how fools mock his name — the same dynamic Hezekiah invokes against Sennacherib.
A century later, elders cite Hezekiah's response to Micah's prophecy as precedent for how kings should respond to prophetic warning.

hist.context: Hezekiah's act of spreading the letter before YHWH in the temple was a formal appeal to the divine King whose throne sat above the cherubim. The gesture acknowledged that the real conflict was between Sennacherib's claims and YHWH's sovereignty. Tirhakah of Egypt (mentioned in v.9) was the Nubian pharaoh whose approach may have drawn Assyrian forces temporarily away from Jerusalem.

sections.header: Verses 21–38 — “The Angel of the LORD Put to Death 185,000”

heb.paragraph: "Then the angel of the LORD (malʾak̅) went out and put to death 185,000 in the Assyrian camp" (v.36). Isaiah sends word to Hezekiah: God has heard. His taunt-song against Sennacherib: "The Virgin Daughter Zion despises you and mocks you" (v.22). "Whom have you ridiculed and blasphemed? Against whom have you raised your voice? Against the Holy One of Israel!" (v.23). God will put his hook in Sennacherib’s nose and turn him back (v.29). The sign: this year eat what grows of itself; next year the same; in the third year, plant and harvest (v.30). A remnant will take root and bear fruit (v.31). Then: the angel strikes. 185,000 dead in one night (v.36). Sennacherib returns to Nineveh and is murdered by his own sons (v.38).

notes.note: "The Virgin Daughter Zion despises you and mocks you. Whom have you ridiculed? The Holy One of Israel!" God’s taunt-song: Assyria’s mockery is redirected. The virgin daughter LAUGHS at the empire.

"I will put my hook in your nose and my bit in your mouth, and I will make you return by the way you came." God as animal-handler: the empire is livestock. The hook-in-nose imagery is Assyria’s own treatment of prisoners turned back on them.

"The angel of the LORD put to death 185,000 in the Assyrian camp." One angel. One night. 185,000. The deliverance Isaiah promised through 30 chapters of preaching. Sennacherib’s own records confirm his withdrawal without conquering Jerusalem.
"His sons killed him with the sword." Sennacherib murdered by his own children. The man who threatened God’s children is destroyed by his own.

refs.note: Both accounts record the angel's strike and Sennacherib's assassination by his sons, confirming the historical outcome.
The psalm celebrates God rebuking warriors so that horse and chariot lie still — language fitting the Assyrian catastrophe.
Ben Sira's praise of Hezekiah recalls how the angel struck the Assyrian camp, showing the event's prominence in later memory.

hist.context: The death of 185,000 Assyrian soldiers in one night remains historically debated. Herodotus records a tradition of mice destroying Assyrian bowstrings (perhaps a plague metaphor). Sennacherib's own annals, while boasting of conquests, never claim to have taken Jerusalem — a telling silence. His assassination in 681 BC by his sons Adrammelech and Sharezer is confirmed by Assyrian records.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVNow Sennacherib received a report that Tirhakah, the king of Cush, was marching out to fight against him. When he heard it, he sent messengers to Hezekiah with this word:ESVNow the king heard concerning Tirhakah king of Cush, “He has set out to fight against you.” And when he heard it, he sent messengers to Hezekiah, saying,NIVWhen King Hezekiah heard this, he tore his clothes and put on sackcloth and went into the temple of the Lord.ESVAs soon as King Hezekiah heard it, he tore his clothes and covered himself with sackcloth and went into the house of the LORD.

rec.text: ""Hezekiah tore his clothes and put on sackcloth and went into the temple." The right response to crisis: mourning, humility, and the house of God. Not the war room but the temple."

rows.label: The Angel Strikes — 37:21–38
rows.text: "The ANGEL put to death 185,000." One night. Sennacherib returns, murdered by his own sons.

hebtext.note: "Hezekiah received the letter and read it. Then he went up to the temple and spread it out (pāras) before the LORD" (v.14). Hezekiah tears his clothes, puts on sackcloth, goes to the temple, and sends for Isaiah (vv.1–2). Isaiah’s response: "Do not be afraid. This is what the LORD says: I will put a spirit in him so that he will hear a report and return to his own country" (vv.6–7). Sennacherib sends a taunting letter (vv.10–13). Hezekiah’s response is the model prayer of crisis: he physically spreads the letter before God’s face (v.14). His prayer: "You alone are God over all kingdoms. Give ear and hear; open your eyes and see" (vv.16, 17). "Deliver us so that all kingdoms may know that you alone, LORD, are God" (v.20).

"Then the angel of the LORD (malʾak̅) went out and put to death 185,000 in the Assyrian camp" (v.36). Isaiah sends word to Hezekiah: God has heard. His taunt-song against Sennacherib: "The Virgin Daughter Zion despises you and mocks you" (v.22). "Whom have you ridiculed and blasphemed? Against whom have you raised your voice? Against the Holy One of Israel!" (v.23). God will put his hook in Sennacherib’s nose and turn him back (v.29). The sign: this year eat what grows of itself; next year the same; in the third year, plant and harvest (v.30). A remnant will take root and bear fruit (v.31). Then: the angel strikes. 185,000 dead in one night (v.36). Sennacherib returns to Nineveh and is murdered by his own sons (v.38).

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: one angel, 185,000, one night. God’s hook in Assyria’s nose. Faith at 9: Hezekiah’s prayer model. The test passed. Everything Isaiah preached vindicated.

coaching.tip: Hezekiah 'spread out the letter before the LORD' (v. 14) — Sennacherib's threatening letter, laid open in the temple as a prayer. The gesture is both literal and symbolic: he makes God read the threat. This becomes a model of prayer across Jewish and Christian tradition — bring the impossible situation into God's presence, unfolded and exposed. Don't summarize the problem; show it to God.

God's response to Sennacherib (vv. 22–29) is poetry — mockery, in fact. 'The Virgin Daughter Zion despises you and mocks you' (v. 22). The most powerful army on earth is laughed at by an unwalled city because God fights for her. Then 185,000 Assyrian soldiers die overnight (v. 36). Isaiah doesn't explain the mechanism; the point is theological, not military. God's mockery precedes God's deliverance.
